summary:
South Korea's economic transformation from one of the world's poorest countries in the aftermath of the [[event:2668|Korean War]] to a major industrialized nation by the 1990s is known as the Miracle on the Han River. In the early 1960s South Korea had a per capita income lower than many African countries. Under the authoritarian government of Park Chung-hee, who took power in a 1961 coup, an export-oriented industrialization strategy was implemented with state direction of investment toward large industrial conglomerates (chaebol) including Hyundai, Samsung, and LG.

Growth rates averaging over eight percent annually through the 1960s, 1970s, and 1980s were driven by manufactured exports, heavy industry investment, and a highly educated workforce. South Korea progressed through successive stages of industrial development, moving from textiles and simple manufacturing to steel, shipbuilding, electronics, and eventually high-technology products. The country became a major automobile producer and electronics exporter. By the 1990s South Korea had achieved developed-country income levels and was admitted to the OECD in 1996.

The model's social costs included suppression of labor rights and political opposition under authoritarian governance, and concentration of economic power in family-controlled chaebol. Democratic transition occurred in 1987 following mass protests. The 1997–1998 [[event:2769|Asian financial crisis]] severely affected South Korea, requiring an IMF bailout and forcing significant economic restructuring, but the country recovered relatively rapidly.
